mnemonics.title: POLYMORPHISM Meaning
mnemonics.content: Poly = Many, Morph = Forms. A single interface (function call) behaves differently based on WHAT object it's called on.

mnemonics.title: Compile vs Runtime Types
mnemonics.content: COMPILE-TIME: Overloading (same name, different params) → FAST, resolved early. RUNTIME: Overriding (virtual functions) → FLEXIBLE, resolved via vtable.

mnemonics.title: Virtual Function Rule
mnemonics.content: If base declares function as VIRTUAL, derived can OVERRIDE it. Without 'virtual', derived HIDES (not overrides). Remember: VIRTUAL = DYNAMIC BINDING.

mnemonics.title: Pure Virtual = Abstract
mnemonics.content: virtual void speak() = 0; This makes class ABSTRACT - cannot create objects, MUST be overridden by derived classes. Think: =0 means 'pay to play' (must implement).

mnemonics.title: Virtual Destructor Rule
mnemonics.content: If class has virtual functions, ALWAYS make destructor virtual! Otherwise: derived part won't be cleaned up when deleting through base pointer.

acronyms.usage: Lookup table compiler creates for each polymorphic class. Contains pointers to virtual function implementations.
Hidden pointer stored in each object of a polymorphic class. Points to the vtable of that class's actual type.
Mechanism that identifies object type at runtime (used by dynamic_cast and typeid). Enabled when polymorphic base has at least one virtual function.
Idiom to hide implementation details; often uses polymorphism to swap implementations at runtime.

visualTips.visualization: Draw a base class 'Shape' at top with draw() as virtual. Below, draw 'Circle', 'Square', 'Triangle' inheriting from Shape. Show ONE base pointer pointing to each shape, calling draw() - same code, different outputs (circle draws circle, square draws square).
Draw object boxes with VPTR inside pointing to a VTABLE box. VTABLE contains function pointer slots: [0]=Base::func, [1]=Derived::override. When calling virtual, follow VPTR→VTABLE→function address.
Draw a TV remote (BASE POINTER) with buttons (VIRTUAL FUNCTIONS). Different TVs (DERIVED OBJECTS) respond differently to same button press - same remote, different behaviors.

analogies.analogy: Your TV remote has a 'Power' button. Press it with TV plugged in → TV turns on. Press it with AC plugged in → AC turns on. Same button (function call), different device (object type) = different behavior. That's polymorphism!

Menu (BASE CLASS) lists 'food' as virtual. Kitchen (DERIVED CLASSES) decides what to cook: Chinese kitchen makes dim sum, Italian makes pizza. Customer orders 'food' → gets different dish based on kitchen type.

Contract says 'You MUST have skill()' (= pure virtual). Can't hire a generic 'Employee' - you must hire a specific 'Developer', 'Designer', etc. who implements skill(). Abstract class defines WHAT, derived defines HOW.

analogies.analoy: Use 'override' after derived function declaration. Compiler checks: 'Does this actually override a virtual function?' If not, ERROR. Like spell-check for inheritance - prevents silent failures if base function signature changes.
